summary.intent: Break the monolithic Coverage CI job into parallel shards — 8 native nextest partition jobs plus 6 per-package wasm jobs — merging all LCOV artifacts in a final gate job to reduce wall-clock CI time.
summary.outcome: Implemented the full sharding pipeline: new `cargo xtask coverage native` CLI with optional `--partition hash:K/N`, three-job GitHub Actions topology (coverage-native matrix, coverage-wasm matrix, Coverage merge/check/CRAP/Codecov), updated spec docs, and committed to branch `codex/sharded-coverage-ci` after passing the pre-push gate.

repo: The repo already had `cargo xtask coverage merge` that unions LCOV hits without double-counting, making artifact-based sharding straightforward.
`xtask::coverage::default_wasm_coverage_targets()` owns the canonical list of 6 wasm packages — the workflow matrix should derive from this rather than hardcoding.
The final CI job must stay named exactly `Coverage` to avoid branch-protection rule churn.
CRAP analysis and Codecov upload must live in the post-merge job because they consume the fully merged lcov.info, not partial shards.
`cargo xci --profile fast` is the required pre-push gate; it includes native coverage with thresholds and is the last stage, making it the slow part of the fast profile.
`cargo xfmt` invokes `dx fmt` broadly across Dioxus files, not just Rust — formatter diffs should be inspected after running it.

Workspace-wide nextest partitioning is safer than package-group splits for native coverage because proc-macro and cross-crate coverage (e.g. `ars-derive`) is only produced when other crates' tests run.

code.finding: Existing wasm coverage helpers showed the pattern (options struct → arg builder fn → caller) used to model the new NativeCoverageOptions + native_coverage_args.
Original monolithic coverage job: native workspace run, six sequential wasm packages, threshold check, CRAP, upload — all in one job. Replaced by three jobs.
The serial `cargo xtask ci coverage` path was refactored to call the new native coverage helper without a partition, keeping local parity with the sharded CI behavior.
CI spec had stale job names, a phantom `test-harness` job example, and a dependency graph that didn't reflect the sharded topology — all updated during the post-implementation audit.

workflow: Test filters for `cargo test -p xtask --lib` require the exact test function name, not the module path prefix — `coverage::native_coverage_args` matched zero tests, but `native_coverage_args` worked.
Red-green TDD against pure argument construction functions lets you validate CI command shape without paying for instrumented workspace runs.
Synthetic LCOV files (two lines, two source records) are sufficient to smoke-test `coverage merge` and `coverage check-all` CLI paths outside unit tests.
A post-implementation audit pass (spec/workflow drift, stale references, dependency graph) caught real inconsistencies in `14-ci.md` that would have misled future contributors.

friction: `cargo xci --profile fast` ran for an extended period requiring many polling cycles — trybuild tests and `ars-core` compile-contract tests were the slow tail.
Skill path aliases in the agent prompt pointed to non-existent filesystem locations, requiring a fallback to `~/.claude/skills/` copies before work could begin.
The initial test filter `cargo test -p xtask --lib coverage::native_coverage_args` silently matched zero tests instead of erroring, masking whether red tests were actually red.
open_items: Tune the native partition count (currently 8) based on actual CI run times after the first PR run — one shard may dominate and warrant re-balancing.
Full sharded coverage workload was not run locally; correctness of the 14-artifact merge and per-threshold checks will only be confirmed by the CI run on the PR.
The wasm matrix still hardcodes the 6 package names in the YAML rather than deriving them from `default_wasm_coverage_targets()` — a future improvement would auto-generate the matrix.
